model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Duke is the #1 overall team in the country with a staggering +39.17 adjusted efficiency margin, roughly 14 points per 100 possessions higher than St. John's +24.8, featuring the nation's best defense (AdjD 88.7) and a top-4 offense (AdjO 127.9) that should challenge even St. John's excellent 12th-ranked defense. While Duke is dealing with significant injuries—starting point guard Caleb Foster is out after surgery and Isaiah Evans was hurt during the ACC Tournament—the Blue Devils have continued to dominate with Cayden Boozer stepping into an expanded role, and their 31-2 record reflects a team with the depth and talent to absorb these losses. St. John's enters with tremendous momentum from their Big East Tournament championship run behind Zuby Ejiofor and Rick Pitino's coaching, but the roughly 14-point efficiency margin gap is simply too large for even a well-coached, defensively elite 5-seed to overcome against a historically dominant Duke squad.

reasoningStep.title: East Sweet 16 1: Duke vs St. John's
reasoningStep.summary: Duke's historically elite efficiency profile (+39.17 AdjEM, #1 nationally) creates a massive quality gap over St. John's (+24.8, #20), and while Duke's injuries to Caleb Foster, Isaiah Evans, and others are concerning, the Blue Devils have continued winning convincingly. St. John's elite defense and Big East Tournament momentum under Pitino give them a credible upset path, but the ~14-point efficiency margin gap and Duke's depth make the Blue Devils a strong favorite.
evidence: Duke: KenPom #1, AdjEM +39.17, AdjO 127.9 (4th), AdjD 88.7 (1st), 31-2 record
St. John's: KenPom #20, AdjEM +24.8, AdjO 119.9 (46th), AdjD 95.1 (12th), 27-6 record
Duke missing Caleb Foster (surgery, out for foreseeable future), Isaiah Evans (injured ACC Tournament), Patrick Ngongba II also injured—three key pieces down
Cayden Boozer stepped into expanded role and Duke kept winning through injuries
St. John's won Big East Tournament beating UConn 72-52; Zuby Ejiofor is Big East Player of the Year and Defensive Player of the Year
Cameron Boozer vs Zuby Ejiofor is a marquee individual matchup in the post
~14.4 point adjusted efficiency margin gap is very large even for a 1-vs-5 seed matchup
Rick Pitino's tournament pedigree gives St. John's a credible but insufficient upset path

Our expert thinks so. Jacob Kupferman / Getty Images By Brendan Marks March 17, 2026 Updated 3:21 pm EDT Share full article 46 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Let’s try this again. Last postseason, I picked Duke — the top team in KenPom’s rankings at the time, led by a freshman Wooden Award winner — to win the national title, beating a fellow No. 1 seed in the title game. That prediction looked good for a while, until the Blue Devils’ all-time collapse to Houston in the Final Four . Instead of hanging a sixth banner in Durham, that team earned an infamous honorific: one of the best teams ever to not win it all. Advertisement But this year, I’m running it back. Give me Duke — again the top team in KenPom’s rankings, led by the likely freshman Wooden Award winner — to cut down the nets in April. And, yeah, I’m even doubling down on my prediction that Jon Scheyer’s team sees another No. 1 seed on the final Monday of this season. My rationale, much like last March, starts with Duke’s generational freshman superstar: Cameron Boozer, who leads the Blue Devils — like Cooper Flagg a year ago — in almost every major statistical category. But beyond the future top-three NBA Draft pick, this Duke squad is the definition of sum over parts. result.text: Duke Keeps on Winning as It Adjusts to an Injury-Depleted Lineup - Hoops HQ HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Bracketology & Bubble Watch Rankings Login Subscribe Your All-Access Pass to College Basketball HoopsHQ Login Subscribe HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Rankings Transfer portal Recruiting Betting & Picks Bracketology & Bubble Watch NBA Draft News Long Reads Advertise Terms & Conditions Privacy Policy ACC Duke Keeps on Winning as It Adjusts to an Injury-Depleted Lineup With three key pieces down for the count, Duke keeps finding new ways to win. Credit a versatile roster and a renaissance for Cayden Boozer. Brett Friedlander 0 Gift This Article Published: March 14, 2026 7:13 am EDT With three key pieces injured, Duke keeps finding new ways to win Getty Images CHARLOTTE, N.C. – Isaiah Evans bit on a shot fake by Clemson’s Butta Johnson and launched high into the air with five minutes remaining in the first half of Duke’s ACC Tournament semifinal game against the Tigers on Friday. As he returned to the floor, the Duke shooting guard crashed into Johnson and both fell hard onto the Spectrum Center court. The collision caused everyone on the Blue Devils bench to leap up with worried looks on their faces. Their concern was understandable. Just one year ago on this same court, they lost star Cooper Flagg for the rest of the tournament with ankle injury. This time, their concern was quickly eased when Evans, who saved the nation’s top-ranked team by scoring a career-high 32 points a day earlier in a one-point quarterfinal win against Florida State, got up almost immediately with no hint of discomfort. But the play and the reaction stressed an important reality as Duke moves into Saturday’s championship showdown with Virginia and beyond. With two starters already sidelined, the last thing the Blue Devils need right now is another injury to another key player. So far, they’ve been able to carry on without point guard Caleb Evans, who will be out for several weeks with a fractured foot, and big man Patrick Ngongba, who is expected back for the NCAA Tournament with a less serious foot issue. After beating Clemson 73-61, they’re one win away from their conference-record 24th tournament title and third in coach Jon Scheyer’s four seasons since succeeding his mentor Mike Krzyzewski. “I mean it’s March, so it’s not really time for any excuses,” Evans said. “We’ve just got to figure out how to execute Duke basketball offensively and defensively.” There have been times over the past two nights that the Blue Devils have done just that. They looked absolutely dominant during a 19-2 spurt midway through the second half against Florida State on Thursday. And they were even better for the final 7:44 of a first half in which they broke the game open by holding Clemson without a field goal while outscoring the Tigers 22-4. But because of a depleted seven-ma

Our expert thinks so. Jacob Kupferman / Getty Images By Brendan Marks March 17, 2026 Updated 3:21 pm EDT Share full article 46 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Let’s try this again. Last postseason, I picked Duke — the top team in KenPom’s rankings at the time, led by a freshman Wooden Award winner — to win the national title, beating a fellow No. 1 seed in the title game. That prediction looked good for a while, until the Blue Devils’ all-time collapse to Houston in the Final Four . Instead of hanging a sixth banner in Durham, that team earned an infamous honorific: one of the best teams ever to not win it all. Advertisement But this year, I’m running it back. Give me Duke — again the top team in KenPom’s rankings, led by the likely freshman Wooden Award winner — to cut down the nets in April. And, yeah, I’m even doubling down on my prediction that Jon Scheyer’s team sees another No. 1 seed on the final Monday of this season. My rationale, much like last March, starts with Duke’s generational freshman superstar: Cameron Boozer, who leads the Blue Devils — like Cooper Flagg a year ago — in almost every major statistical category. But beyond the future top-three NBA Draft pick, this Duke squad is the definition of sum over parts. It still has NBA talent — maybe not so much that every starter will be drafted in June, like last year’s team — but its strength is its role definition, the

result.text: Duke Keeps on Winning as It Adjusts to an Injury-Depleted Lineup - Hoops HQ HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Bracketology & Bubble Watch Rankings Login Subscribe Your All-Access Pass to College Basketball HoopsHQ Login Subscribe HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Rankings Transfer portal Recruiting Betting & Picks Bracketology & Bubble Watch NBA Draft News Long Reads Advertise Terms & Conditions Privacy Policy ACC Duke Keeps on Winning as It Adjusts to an Injury-Depleted Lineup With three key pieces down for the count, Duke keeps finding new ways to win. Credit a versatile roster and a renaissance for Cayden Boozer. Brett Friedlander 0 Gift This Article Published: March 14, 2026 7:13 am EDT With three key pieces injured, Duke keeps finding new ways to win Getty Images CHARLOTTE, N.C. – Isaiah Evans bit on a shot fake by Clemson’s Butta Johnson and launched high into the air with five minutes remaining in the first half of Duke’s ACC Tournament semifinal game against the Tigers on Friday. As he returned to the floor, the Duke shooting guard crashed into Johnson and both fell hard onto the Spectrum Center court. The collision caused everyone on the Blue Devils bench to leap up with worried looks on their faces. Their concern was understandable. Just one year ago on this same court, they lost star Cooper Flagg for the rest of the tournament with ankle injury. This time, their concern was quickly eased when Evans, who saved the nation’s top-ranked team by scoring a career-high 32 points a day earlier in a one-point quarterfinal win against Florida State, got up almost immediately with no hint of discomfort. But the play and the reaction stressed an important reality as Duke moves into Saturday’s championship showdown with Virginia and beyond. With two starters already sidelined, the last thing the Blue Devils need right now is another injury to another key player. So far, they’ve been able to carry on without point guard Caleb Evans, who will be out for several weeks with a fractured foot, and big man Patrick Ngongba, who is expected back for the NCAA Tournament with a less serious foot issue. After beating Clemson 73-61, they’re one win away from their conference-record 24th tournament title and third in coach Jon Scheyer’s four seasons since succeeding his mentor Mike Krzyzewski. “I mean it’s March, so it’s not really time for any excuses,” Evans said. “We’ve just got to figure out how to execute Duke basketball offensively and defensively.” There have been times over the past two nights that the Blue Devils have done just that. They looked absolutely dominant during a 19-2 spurt midway through the second half against Florida State on Thursday. And they were even better for the final 7:44 of a first half in which they broke the game open by holding Clemson without a field goal while outscoring the Tigers 22-4. But because of a depleted seven-ma

modelTrace.content:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize the key findings:

**Duke (1-seed):**
- KenPom #1, Torvik #1, 31-2 record
- AdjEM: +39.17 (KenPom), AdjO 127.9 (4th), AdjD 88.7 (best in nation)
- Led by Cameron Boozer (Player of the Year candidate), Cayden Boozer
- Key injuries: Caleb Foster (starting PG) out for "foreseeable future" after surgery; Isaiah Evans also injured during ACC Tournament; Patrick Ngongba II also mentioned as injured
- Despite three key injuries, Duke kept winning through ACC Tournament and early NCAA rounds
- Cayden Boozer stepped up significantly with Foster out

**St. John's (5-seed):**
- KenPom #20, Torvik #18, 27-6 record
- AdjEM: +24.8, AdjO 119.9 (46th), AdjD 95.1 (12th nationally)
- Led by Zuby Ejiofor (Big East Player of the Year AND Defensive Player of the Year), RJ Luis (Big East POY from prior picks)
- Won Big East Tournament, beating UConn 72-52 in championship
- Rick Pitino coaching, tremendous momentum
- Elite defense (12th nationally)

**Key matchup dynamics:**
- Duke's efficiency margin (+39.17) vs St. John's (+24.8) = ~14.4 point gap
- Duke's elite offense (127.9) vs St. John's elite defense (95.1) is the marquee matchup
- St. John's offense (119.9) vs Duke's historically great defense (88.7)
- Duke is dealing with significant injuries (Foster, Evans, Ngongba) but has still been dominant
- Cameron Boozer vs Zuby Ejiofor is a premier individual matchup
- Even with injuries, Duke's talent and efficiency gap is enormous

  "pick": {
    "gameId": "s16-east-1",
    "winnerId": "duke",
    "confidence": 0.87,

While Duke is dealing with significant injuries—starting point guard Caleb Foster is out after surgery, and Isaiah Evans was hurt during the ACC Tournament—the Blue Devils have continued to dominate with Cayden Boozer stepping into an expanded role, and their 31-2 record reflects a team with the depth and talent to absorb these losses. St. John's enters with tremendous momentum from their Big East Tournament championship run behind Zuby Ejiofor (Big East Player and Defensive Player of the Year) and Rick Pitino's coaching, but the roughly 14-point efficiency margin gap is simply too large for even a well-coached, defensively elite 5-seed to overcome against a historically dominant Duke squad."
  },

St. John's elite defense and Big East Tournament momentum under Pitino give them a credible upset path, but the ~14-point efficiency margin gap and Duke's depth make the Blue Devils a strong favorite.",
